topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a dropped stone. My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers hovering over the screen as if they might burn the paper. The headline, which I had painstakingly crafted, was undeniably mine. But the byline? It bore the name of a complete stranger. A cold wash of disbelief swept over me, quickly followed by a rising heat in my cheeks. I reread the text, scanning the paragraphs for any misplaced signature, any slip-up that might explain this monumental error. Every word felt scrutinized, every comma a potential trap. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my office, the cheap carpet muffling the sound of my hurried steps. My breath hitched in short, shallow bursts. Who would do this? The sheer audacity of it made my hands shake, and I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles turned white.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my vision blurring slightly. The words confirmed the nightmare: my nuanced analysis, my unique perspective, had been presented as the work of someone else. A slow, creeping dread began to coil around my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning softly beneath my weight, and let my gaze drift across the empty room, seeking an escape route that didn't exist. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the dial pad, but the impulse to call someone felt paralyzed, overridden by a paralyzing sense of disorientation. I pressed my palms flat against the cool surface of the desk, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, unsettling lurch in my core. The silence in the room seemed amplified, heavy and suffocating.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, a sudden, sharp intake of air escaped my lungs. My hands, resting on the keyboard, began to tremble uncontrollably. I forced myself to look again, my eyes darting back and forth between the published piece and the attribution listed beneath it. A wave of icy apprehension washed over me, chilling me to the bone despite the stuffy air in the room. I felt a strange pressure building behind my eyes, a frantic need to verify, to disprove this impossible reality. I pushed away from my workstation, the chair scraping loudly against the floor. Each movement felt exaggerated, clumsy. I walked to the window, peering out at the indifferent cityscape, my whole body vibrating with an internal alarm signal. I could feel the blood pounding rhythmically in my ears, a frantic drumbeat against the sudden, vast emptiness of the situation.
